title_tesim: Page of article, “Beirut to Bagdad,” circa 1928
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1928. Beirut to Bagdad. Bartlett story. Touring Topics. Photocopy pages. 5.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: In 1928, Adelbert Bartlett wrote and contributed photographs to an article documenting his trip through Lebanon, Syria and Iraq. The work was published as, “Beirut to Bagdad,” Touring Topics, 1928: 32-35, 60. The magazine, started in 1909 (now called Westways) was a publication for members of the Automobile Club of Southern California.

Photograph of page 33 of Adelbert Bartlett’s article, “Beirut to Bagdad: A Southern Californian motors through the land of the Arabian Nights.” The page describes some of the people accompanying Bartlett on his trip as well as provides his own reasons for attempting the trek. Text includes three photos: Zeitoomi women of Southern Turkey demonstrating yarn spinning; a Nazareth inhabitant exiting a mosque; a pack of camels sharing the road with an American automobile.
rights_statement_tesim: http://vocabs.library.ucla.edu/rights/copyrighted
human_readable_rights_statement_tesim: copyrighted

title_tesim: Image of Dr. Cass Arthur Reed, Mrs. Albert L. Bagnall, and others, 1928
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2369 P1 to 9, 2370 P1 to 5, Near East Colleges, Dr. Cass A. Reed, Mrs. A.L. Bagnall, 951 Western, also Claremont -Pomona College
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Dr. Cass Arthur Reed was a graduate of Pomona College, and then later, Harvard, Columbia and the Union Theological Seminary. He earned degrees from all of these institutions. In 1912, he was appointed the Dean of the International College of Smyrna Turkey. He is referenced in numerous L.A. Times articles, including the Aug. 11, 1914 article "Pomona 'Grad' College Dean Home from Smyrna, Will Take Sports Leader with Him to Turkey".
This image is a group photo including Dr. Reed and likely Mrs. A.L. Bagnall, both sitting to the right of the group.

title_tesim: Page of Article "A Californian in the Holy Land", circa 1929
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1929 Beirut to Jerusalem, Bartlett copies pages, Beirut-Jerusalem.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: On this page are images of the city of Nazareth and two boys working as carpenters.

This image is a copy of a page of an article, "A Californian in the Holy Land", written by Adelbert Bartlett in 1929. This article covers his trip through Beirut, Jerusalem, and Palestine. He traveled with a professor, who is not named. Various locals guided them on their trip, as documented in article. Once arriving in Beirut via ship, they traveled primarily by car.

title_tesim: Lobby of the Windemere Hotel, interior design by Dean Train, Santa Monica, circa 1955
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: John Lindsay
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Photograph of the Windemere Hotel lobby, refurbished by Dean Train, interior designer. View shows multiple seating areas delineated by a beam ceiling and dark-colored pillars accented with gold molding. Velvet furnishings include a circular bench, lounge chairs, and a sofa. A marble fireplace can me seen in the background. The material is used again at the telephone station (seen at foreground). Red patterned wallpaper, chandelier, metal sculptures and wall paintings add to the décor.
The "before" photograph of this space features architect John Lindsay (see image ark no. 21198/zz002d3zj6)
Note from interior designer Dean Train to Adelbert Bartlett relating to this image(see "Relation" note).

description_tesim: Shirley R. Doman married John Axel Michelson in 1946.
This image is of Miss. Shirley R. Doman. She poses looking towards the right, with her left hand on her waist. She wears a dark dress with a lace collar. Her hair is rolled in a common 1940s style.

Miss Shirley R. Doman was the daughter of Mr. and Mrs. Roberta Shaw Doman of Pacific Palisades. She attended University High School and then UCLA and was an Alpha Chi Omega sorority sister. She married John Axel Michelson in 1946.

description_tesim: This image is of Miss. Shirley R. Doman. She poses smiling with one hand on her waist, the other rests on a table next to her. She wears a dark dress with a lace collar. Her hair is rolled in a common 1940s style.
Shirley R. Doman married John Axel Michelson in 1946.

Miss Shirley R. Doman was the daughter of Mr. and Mrs. Roberta Shaw Doman of Pacific Palisades. She attended University High School and then UCLA and was an Alpha Chi Omega sorority sister. She married John Axel Michelson in 1946.

title_tesim: Two images of ships in a harbor, copy prints, 1934
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2973, 9/[…]/34. John Crum. Old sailing ships (Mrs. Capt Morris). Also steamer on Pac. Coast, 1900’s.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Two copy print images photographed upside down and right-side up. The photos are of similar ships docked in a harbor. At top: a small sail boat passes in front of a  ship with three sailing masts. This may be a steamship (a smoke stack is visible at center). At bottom: A three-masted schooner or clipper ship in the harbor. Two small tugboats can be seen at both ends of the ship along with additional boats seen in the harbor. Multiple buildings are viewed in the background, along with a large crane and a row of derricks.
John H. Crum was an avid collector of family memorabilia and other items, many of which were photographed by Adelbert Bartlett.

title_tesim: Opera singer Julian Oliver and fellow singers (?) dressed as characters from “La Tosca,” Del Mar Club, Santa Monica, 1942
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 2896 P 2
Text from negative sleeve: 2896 P 1-8. Del Mar Club, Santa Monica, 1942. Fashion Show, Opera La Tosco [sic] 10/20/42. Julian Oliver 1725 N. Bronson, 1 set 8x10 DW Holly.
named_subject_tesim: Casa del Mar Hotel (Santa Monica, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Ensemble photograph featuring tenor Julian Oliver with three performers, dressed as characters from the opera “La Tosca.” Oliver (at left wearing a buttoned overcoat with visible lace cuffs, and boots) is portraying the artist Mario Cavaradossi.  He looks directly into the camera. Standing next to him is a woman portraying Floria Tosca (Cavaradossi’s lover). The faraway look in her eyes is countered by the man to her right, likely the Baron Scarpia (holding a bicorn hat and wearing a powdered wig, overcoat, waistcoat [vest], breaches and stockings). He gazes directly at her while a third man, dressed as a soldier wearing an overcoat and sash at his waist, leans in next to him. The performers stand on a stage or concrete platform. The walls behind them are covered with tufted fabric. Double exposure overlaps this image with that of two men wearing contemporary suits and ties standing behind a woman seated in a chair. A staircase can be seen behind them at top right. Ensemble photographed in a room at the Del Mar Club, Santa Monica.
rights_statement_tesim: http://vocabs.library.ucla.edu/rights/copyrighted
combined_subject_ssim: Casa del Mar Hotel (Santa Monica, Calif.)
human_readable_rights_statement_tesim: copyrighted

subject_tesim: Santa Monica Canyon (Los Angeles County, Calif.)
Will Rogers State Beach (Los Angeles, Calif.)
description_tesim: Bartlett photographed this stretch of Pacific Coast Highway from the same vantage point in 1928. Images show slight changes in the landscape over time (see image ark no. 21198/zz002bx9qj).

Bird’s eye view towards Santa Monica Canyon where Chautauqua Blvd. and West Channel Road end at Pacific Coast Highway on the coast, with the ocean, Will Rogers State Beach and the Pacific Palisades cliffs beyond. The lighthouse building, and the Mary Virginia McCormick residence (seen atop the Palisades cliffs at right) are visible in the middle distance. Hotels and businesses are located where the canyon meets the coast. Legible signs on the buildings read: “Milton’s Canyon Building,” and “Liquor Store, Beer Wines Liquors.”

description_tesim: Image of a couple dressed in Persian attire. He wears a bejeweled turban, long embroidered khalat (robe) over stripped sirwal (baggy trousers), and curled-toe slippers. She wears a shear veil that covers most of her costume. They are perched on the edge of a bed covered with silken linens and pillows. They both read from a book he holds. Set props include swaths of fabric pooled on the floor, and potted plants seen behind the couple.

title_tesim: Tex Cooper with performers and attendees of the Will Rogers Memorial Celebration, Santa Monica, 1940
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2901, Will Rogers Celebration, Stage Show - Santa Monica July 25, 26, 27, year 1940.  Will's horse "Soapsuds," Leo Carillo.  At fence:  Tex Cooper in high boots & uniform, Leonard Trainor who takes care of horse, Linda Ware, actress, Carolyn Bartlett
Handwritten on edge of negative: Tex Cooper-Center [top]; 5 [bottom]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Related to Los Angeles Times article, “Tribute Paid Will Rogers: Five Thousand Persons and 1400 Horses March in Santa Monica Parade Santa Monica Honors Rogers Hundred Thousand See Humorist's Favorite Horse in Parade,” 28 Jul. 1940

The Will Rogers Memorial Celebration and Pageant was a three-day event with proceeds going to the Red Cross. In 1940, the event coincided with the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School.

Actor Tex Cooper is flanked by young women and girls, most in costume. Cooper wears a soldier’s overcoat, high boots, a scarf tie and wide-brimmed hat. Some of the girls wear dresses with lace-up bodices; one child wears a plaid shirt and cowboy hat. All are unidentified with the exception of Carolyn Bartlett (second from left) and actress Linda Ware (right of Carolyn). One other male kneels on one knee at left. He wears a cowboy hat, plaid shirt and jeans and holds a rope. Probably photographed at Santa Monica High School.

description_tesim: Related to Los Angeles Times article, “Tribute Paid Will Rogers: Five Thousand Persons and 1400 Horses March in Santa Monica Parade Santa Monica Honors Rogers Hundred Thousand See Humorist's Favorite Horse in Parade,” 28 Jul. 1940

Actress Linda Ware standing next to a line of young men in parade uniform. They wear double-breasted tunics and monogramed shako hats topped with pompoms. They hold rifles at their sides. The actress wears stripped palazzo pants and blouse, an overcoat and a jauntily placed shako hat; a rifle rests on her shoulder. Behind them hangs a painted backdrop of trees. Probably photographed at Santa Monica High School.

The Will Rogers Memorial Celebration and Pageant was a three-day event with proceeds going to the Red Cross. In 1940, the event coincided with the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School.

In 1940, the event coincided with the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. Festivities included a tour of Will Rogers Ranch for convention delegates, a rodeo held at Municipal Stadium, and a nightly pageant held at Santa Monica High School.

Delegates from seven states photographed at the national convention of the Will Rogers Memorial Highway (U.S. 66) Association. description_tesim: Image housed in negative sleeve with other "Rigoletto" images photographed at John Adams Auditorium between November 26-December 4, 1949. This performance took place at Ocean Park Municipal Auditorium.

Santa Monica Civic Opera performance of “Rigoletto.” The singers wear costumes evoking 16th century attire - the female singers wear long gowns with full skirts and puffed sleeves. The men wear stockings, doublet-inspired tunics, ruff collars, and baldrics (for holding a sword) around the waist. Rigoletto (portrayed by Enrico Porta) stands at center with the Duke of Mantua (Raymond Gagan) while the crowd of nobles and courtiers watch the event transpire. The stage set represents a room in the Duke’s palace complete with a throne (an ornate wooden chair covered with a canopy). Large halberds (battle-axes) are propped against walls built with faux columns and decorative panels; crests are mounted onto the walls. Behind these walls is a backdrop painted to look like a stone exterior wall. Photographed from balcony, allowing the full stage, curtains and orchestra to be viewed.
Date range derived from photographed performances of "Rigolleto" represented in the collection.

The Santa Monica Civic Opera performed “Rigoletto” at the John Adams Auditorium November 26-27, and December 3-4.

title_tesim: "Bohème" production, Act 2, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1955
caption_tesim: Handwritten along edge of negative: 14
Text from negative sleeve: #10 to 20-. La Boheme. SM Civic Opera. June 1955. June Moss. B Ludwig.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: A notice for a production of La Bohème, performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 21 Aug. 1955: D6

Opera singers performing in a production of Giacomo Puccini’s, La Bohème. The cast, in period costume, take up the stage. Several children sit or kneel while adult cast members mill about. A singer portraying Rodolfo holds the arm and sings to Mimi (June Moss). The painted backdrop and set props recreate the Café Momus and adjoining buildings. The exterior set also includes a table and chairs (the table set with bottles and glasses), hanging lanterns, and a poster advertising “livres-musique.” Microphones and an amplifier can be viewed at the stage’s edge, while a piano player and violinists are visible in the orchestra pit at foreground. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

description_tesim: Two singers performing in a production of Giacomo Puccini’s opera, La Bohème. The singers, in period costume, portray Rodolfo and Mimi. The painted backdrop and set props recreate an attic apartment in Paris, while windows painted with snow-covered panes suggest the winter season outdoors. Props include a table set with bottles, glasses, a quill pen and ink. Chairs, a market basket, wall painting and easel complete the set. Microphones and an amplifier can be viewed at the stage’s edge, while a piano player and violinists are visible in the orchestra pit at foreground. Performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
A notice for a production of La Bohème, performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 21 Aug. 1955: D6

title_tesim: "Rigoletto" curtain call with Ray Gagan, Enrico Porta and Natalie Garrotto, John Adams Auditorium, Santa Monica, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 148
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.

description_tesim: Opera singers (L to R) Ray Gagan, Enrico Porta, and Natalie Garrotto on stage at curtain call after a Santa Monica Civic Opera performance of “Rigoletto.”  Garrotto, as Gilda, wears a long, off-the-shoulder gown with a satin trimmed neckline and puff sleeves. Porta, as Rigoletto, wears a doublet-inspired stripped tunic, a small ruff collar, full, short breeches (called slops), stockings and a cape; Gagan wears an embellished satin tunic. Musicians can be seen from the orchestra pit at foreground.

The Santa Monica Civic Opera performed “Rigoletto” at the John Adams Auditorium November 26-27, and December 3-4. This is either the November 26th or December 4th performance, with Natalie Garrotto in the role of Gilda. The role alternated between Natalie Garrotto, Kay Marshall and Diana Guadagno.

description_tesim: Opera singer Natalie Garrotto performing in “Rigoletto” in a Santa Monica Civic Opera production. The singer wears a long, off-the-shoulder gown with a satin trimmed neckline and puff sleeves.  She stands near a bench in the middle of a walled garden and mimes knitting with needles and an embroidery hoop. Background flats are painted to mimic stone walls- one with a functional door, another with an open archway.  Shrubbery and vines can be seen on and near outer courtyard walls. Two musicians are seen in the lower right-hand corner from the orchestra pit.

The Santa Monica Civic Opera performed “Rigoletto” at the John Adams Auditorium November 26-27, and December 3-4. This is either the November 26th or December 4th performance, with Natalie Garrotto in the role of Gilda. The role alternated between Natalie Garrotto, Kay Marshall and Diana Guadagno.

description_tesim: A notice for this production of "The Barber of Seville," performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at John Adams Junior High School, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 11 Dec. 1955: E5

Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in, "The Barber of Seville." The singers, wearing 18th century Spanish clothing, portray (left to right) Rosina, Berta, Count Almavida (dressed as a soldier), Figaro (behind him), Bartolo and Basilio. A group of soldiers stand in the background. The players are surrounded by a stage set recreating a room in Bartolo’s home. The set, painted with decorative paneled walls and a faux fireplace, is also decorated with end tables, chairs, a curtained window and candelabras. A pianist is visible from the orchestra pit. Performed at John Adams Junior High School, Santa Monica.

caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 20
Text from negative sleeve: [?] to 25. SM Opera. Cavalleria & Pagliacci. 1955. June Moss.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singers performing the Mass passage in "Cavalleria Rusticana." Cast members in Italian peasant costumes take up the stage. Three men stand at different points behind rows of kneeling women and men. Singers portraying Mamma Lucia (left) and Santuzza (right) are seated. Behind the players, an Italian village is depicted using a painted backdrop and set replete with a wine shop and church. Musicians are visible in the orchestra pit at foreground. Performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.
A notice for this production, conducted by Mario Lanza and performed by the Santa Monica Civic Opera at Barnum Hall, appears in the Los Angeles Times, 7 Aug. 1955: E7
Cavalleria Rustica, a melodrama in one act, was performed in a double-bill with Ruggero Leoncavallo’s opera, Pagliacci, a drama in two acts.

title_tesim: Four girls along an irrigation trench, Fontana, 1930-1931
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2098… 2102–2103–2104–2105–2106–2107–2108–2109–2110–2111–2112. Fontana. Chickens – Corrals. [stamped:] Adelbert Bartlett Los Angeles
Handwritten on edge of negative: 2101
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Fontana was founded in 1913 by A.B. (Azarial Blanchard) Miller, d. 1878-1941. Miller first purchased the 17,000 acres of land in 1906, a year after he began working it. Miller envisioned a booming agricultural town complete with citrus orchards, cattle and poultry farms situated along Route 66. With the formation of the Fontana Land Company in 1914 and Fontana Farms Company in 1918, Miller’s vision began to come to fruition. The Los Angeles Times printed numerous articles regarding A.B. Miller, Fontana Farms and Fontana Land companies from 1914 to 1937; articles specific to poultry farming and egg production date from 1925 to 1931.

Four girls photographed between a row of flowers and an irrigation trench that runs along a residence. The girls wear similar jumpers and sport matching bobbed haircuts. The youngest girl holds a straw hat while the other three wear cloche hats. One stands and three sit along the trench’s edge. A partial view of the residence and a grove of trees can be seen in the background. Photographed on a tract of land in Fontana.

Miller envisioned a booming agricultural town complete with citrus orchards, cattle and poultry farms situated along Route 66. With the formation of the Fontana Land Company in 1914 and Fontana Farms Company in 1918, Miller’s vision began to come to fruition. The Los Angeles Times printed numerous articles regarding A.B. Miller, Fontana Farms and Fontana Land companies from 1914 to 1937; articles specific to poultry farming and egg production date from 1925 to 1931.

Photograph of two boys riding a burro standing posing outside of a log fence or cabin remains. One boy wears a cardigan and tie with a sombrero; the boy seated behind him wears a newsboy cap, riding boots and a tie. The burro is positioned on one of the poured cement steps leading up to a door frame. Possibly photographed in Fontana.

title_tesim: Andrea Karlsen, ballet dancer and co-director of the Ballet des Arts, 1964
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 1964. Ballet La Canada. Music Arts.

description_tesim: Andrea Karlsen, a native of Los Angeles, began her dance studies in New Zealand with Estelle Berre, then studied in Los Angeles with Bronislava Nijinska. After WWII, Nijinska took her to France to join Le Grand Ballets du Marquis de Cuevas, where she attained principal dancer status in 1953. She remained with the company until 1955. She and her husband, the dancer Paul Maure, founded  the Ballet des Arts Company in La Canada in 1957.
Photograph of Andrea Karlsen, ballet dancer and co-director of the Ballet des Arts, posing in a dark-colored classical tutu.

description_tesim: Information about Alicia Mowat as a ballet student provided by newspaper clippings photographed by Bartlett (image ark no. 21198/zz002d5hdr).

As a teenager Alicia Mowat won several beauty pageants in the California (Miss Hollywood, Miss Reseda, Miss Malibu, Miss Huntington Beach, and others). In the 1960's she acted in several Italian films under the name Alicia Brandet (including I ragazzi dell’hully-gully (1964) and Una storia d’amore (1970). She married Giancarl Marcaccini in 1970.

title_tesim: “Il Trovatore” production with Barbara Patton, Tandy ManKenzie and Enrico Porta, Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles, 1950
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 44
Text from negative sleeve: Pace Opera. Il Trovatore 4/22/50
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Verdi’s “Il Trovatore,” produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace, was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, April 22, 1950. The production was critiqued in a Los Angeles Times article, “Verdi Opera Presented,” 24 Apr. 1950: B10.

Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera production of “Il Trovatore,” Act 2, Scene 2. At center stand Manrico and Leonora (portrayed by Tandy MacKenzie and Barbara Patton) - he in a velvet tunic with cape, boots, and wearing a plumed helmet, she in a long gown and veil. Behind her at right stand an attendant and several nuns. Behind Manrico stands the Count di Luna (Enrico Porta, fourth from left) with his attendants. They stand with swords at the ready, wearing helmets, tunics and stockings. The setting is the façade of a convent complete with archways and stained glass windows. Beyond the stage, a portion of a piano can be viewed from the orchestra pit. The opera was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles.

caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 41
Text from negative sleeve: Pace Opera. Il Trovatore 4/22/50
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

description_tesim: Verdi’s “Il Trovatore,” produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace, was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, April 22, 1950. The production was critiqued in a Los Angeles Times article, “Verdi Opera Presented,” 24 Apr. 1950: B10.

Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera production of “Il Trovatore,” Act 3, Scene 1. Cast members dressed as guards of the Count di Luna watch Azucena, a Gypsy woman portrayed by Mina Doerr. They wear helmets with tunics and stockings, and carry swords at their sides. At left stands the Count di Luna, played by Enrico Porta; the Count’s officer, Ferrando (Arnett Amos) stands apart from his soldiers at right. He wears an embellished tunic and cape, denoting his rank above the others. The setting is a camp with a tent nestled under a large tree. The painted backdrop depicts a rocky landscape with few trees. A piano is viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground.  Opera performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles.

caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 9
Text from negative sleeve: Louise Glaum Play Hollywood, 1952. Also Peggy O’ Connell, Otis Garth.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Louise Glaum produced and directed “O.K. By Me,” a comedy written by Sheldon Shedard. Glaum was an actress, drama teacher and director who was active in the Los Angeles area for over 60 years. Originally known as one of the first “vamp” actresses, she later opened several theaters in Los Angeles and an acting school.
Cast member Otis Garth, who portrayed the lead protagonist, Nelson Withers in “O.K. By Me,” photographed on set.
This production was referenced in two Los Angeles Times articles: “Stage Farce Offered at Beaux Arts Theater,” 11 Oct. 1952, and “Beaux Arts Will Reopen,” 19 Sep. 1952.

description_tesim: Verdi’s “Il Trovatore,” produced by Francesco (Frank) Pace, was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, April 22, 1950. The production was critiqued in a Los Angeles Times article, “Verdi Opera Presented,” 24 Apr. 1950: B10.

Los Angeles Civic Grand Opera production of “Il Trovatore,” Act 2, Scene 1. Manrico, portrayed by Tandy Mackenzie, sings to Azucena, a Gypsy woman (Mina Doerr). The setting is a gypsies’ camp. Set props include a kettle and mock fire, anvils set on tree stumps, wine/water jugs and goblets.  Azucena sits on a log next to Manrico’s plumed helmet and sword. Behind them, a painted backdrop depicts a rocky terrain with few trees. Beyond the stage, musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit along with several audience members. The opera was performed at the Wilshire Ebell Theatre, Los Angeles.

title_tesim: International Sourdough Reunion banquet attendees, Hotel Alexandria, Los Angeles, 1949
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: #11
Text from negative sleeve: 2907. Yukon Society Convention 1949. Wm H Brown L.A.
named_subject_tesim: Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Men and women wearing suits and ties or dresses, photographed as they enter a room with tables set for dining. The men and women are attending an International Sourdough Reunion banquet, hosted by the Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California, Hotel Alexandria, Los Angeles.

International Sourdough Reunion, Inc. is a non-profit association of fraternal organizations that share the pioneer history of Alaska and the Yukon in common. Each year, one organization hosts a convention of members from groups located as far north as Fairbanks, Alaska, and as far south as San Diego, California (with points in between). In 1949, the Alaska Yukon Club of Southern California hosted the 18th annual International Sourdough Reunion convention. Events were held at the Hotel Alexandria in Los Angeles. The four-day event (August 17-20) was noted in the article, "Gold Rush is On: Tales of '98 Relived at Sourdough Meeting," Los Angeles Times, 18 Aug. 1949: A2.

title_tesim: Dr. St. Louis Albert Estes on a rock outcropping at Ribbonwood resort, Mountain Center vicinity, 1937
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2803. Ribbonwood Resort. San Jacinto Mts Via Keen Camp Post Office. Wn S. Howell Jr. proprietor. Photos of Ribbonwood Trees, 10/1/37. Dr. St. Louis Estes. Jean Myras.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
subject_tesim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)
description_tesim: Dr. St. Louis Albert Estes (1876-1951) was an American doctor and the author of Raw Food and Health. He lived in Van Nuys, California in the late 1920s and early 1930s, and had 3 children with his first wife in the 1910s, 12 children with his second wife in the 1920s and 1930s.

Dr. St. Louis Albert Estes, wearing a pull-over sweater and seersucker pants, rests against a rock outcropping. He holds a pencil and paper and is starting a list with the title, “How to Live 150 Years.” Ribbonwood trees, shrubs and outcroppings populate the landscape at middle ground, leading up to mountain peaks shaded by clouds. Photographed on the property of the Ribbonwood Resort.

The Ribbonwood Resort, located at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains, was owned and operated by W.S. (Wilson Stout) Howell, Jr., d.1888-1967. Howell purchased 2000 acres of land at the base of the Santa Rosa Mountains from the Southern Pacific Railroad with the intention of farming, but when the Pines-to-Palms Highway opened in 1932, he decided to open a rest stop to capitalize on the traffic that ran through portions of his property. Soon after, he began to clear paths to trails leading up to the Santa Rosa Mountains and created picnic areas. Rustic-style cabins soon followed. By 1935, Howell’s land was a full-fledged resort. Howell’s intention was to offer a place where those in ill health could rest and recuperate in a natural environment. He believed that the mountain location helped him rebuild his health and that it could do the same for others. Howell sold his land in 1964. It was developed into the town of Spring Crest, which sits within the Santa Rosa – San Jacinto Mountains National Monument.

combined_subject_ssim: Santa Rosa and San Jacinto Mountains National Monument (Calif.)

caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2926. Santa Monica, City of. Display of model beach city, 1948. Miss Smith model. USC project.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: City planning exhibit includes an architectural model sitting at ground level; information panels displayed at eye level. Panels describe planning for neighborhoods, shopping centers and recreational facilities. [Header above panels:] Principals of Planning. [Panels 1 and 2:] Neighborhoods: Schools – Maximum walking distance ¼ mile; Maximum enrollment 1000 students; Adequate playgrounds and parks; North lighted classrooms; Outdoor classrooms. Streets – Separation of pedestrian and automotive traffic; Access to schools without crossing service streets; Essential services: Power. Water. Sewers and gas; Off street parking. Housing - Controlled population density; Adequate sunlight; Privacy; Outdoor living; Adequate standards for living. Parks – Open landscaped area; play space for all ages. [Panel 3] District Shopping Center: Serves two or more neighborhoods; Retails household necessities; Accessible to consumers; Separation of service and shoppers; Adequate parking; Readily expandable. [Panels 4 and 5 subject headings indecipherable; includes diagram and illegible bullet points] [Panels 6 and 7:] Recreation and Amusement: Natural site location; Accessibility to transportation from extended areas; Provisions for resort trade and local residents; Segregated facilities for all ages; Open spaces and rest areas in commercial amusement center; Accessibility to beach; Development of beach; Development of Yacht Harbor [“Recreation and Amusement” concludes with a diagram on panel 7].

description_tesim: Trollies carry passengers along the pathway amidst throngs of pedestrians. Their attire varies - from 1920s era bathing suits, to dresses worn with cloche hats and suits worn with boater hats, fedoras and caps. The path follows the sand where multitudes gather, converse and relax under a sea of beach umbrellas. A sign advertising umbrellas can be viewed in the top left-hand quadrant; across, an American flag flies. Beyond the sand lies the ocean, waves cresting, dotted with people. Photographed at Lick Pier (near Ocean Park Pier).
Lick Pier was mostly in Venice, Ocean Park Pier in Santa Monica (Cf. http://www.westland.net/venicehistory/articles/pickpier.htm). View is from Venice side.

subject_tesim: Little Bighorn, Battle of the, Mont., 1876
description_tesim: Photograph of a newspaper clipping accompanied by a reprinted photograph. The article offers a review of "The Custer Myth," written by Col. W.A. Graham (published 1953). The book focuses on General George A. Custer's defeat at Little Big Horn. At right, is a photograph of Custer, seated, surrounded by Indian scouts during the Black Hills Expedition of 1874. The man pointing at the map is named Bloody Knife (Sioux-Arikara Indian), scout for the U.S. 7th Cavalry Regiment and a favorite of Custer. The men were photographed by William Illingworth.

description_tesim: Melville Rapp's business address was 1729 Wilshire Blvd., Santa Monica, CA.

Photograph of a letter from Melville B. Rapp to Santa Monica property owners.  The latter states that in 1922 realtor Melville Rapp and his wife, Beulah, sold housing tracts in Santa Monica between 14th and 17th streets, and Montana Avenue and Carlyle, retaining rights to the water, oil, naptha, gas and other kindered substances deposited in, lying under of flowing through the properties. The letter was apparently sent to all of the tract owners, including Adelbert Bartlett who lived on 15th St., offering to sell those rights for $25 per tract. The photograph also includes a page apparently from the sale document for Bartlett's house on which the oil and other rights are declared.

caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: John Lindsay. Windemere.

description_tesim: The Hotel Windemere was located at 1431 Ocean Ave. Built in 1909, it was demolished in 1962. John Lindsay, a southern California architect, owned the building from at least 1954 until its demolition. According to newspaper reports from 1954 to 1956, he and Jane Wooster Scott were a couple.

Photograph of a room with 5 paintings in an American naïve style. Signed "Wooster," the paintings are early works by Jane Wooster Scott (b. 1929). The paintings hang on a wall covered in striped wall paper above a sofa and end tables at th4e Windemere Hotel.

title_tesim: Christmas wreath on John LaGatta's door, Santa Monica, circa 1942
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: Decorative Designs. Flower arrangement. Girl & minaret (Miss Ohrtland). Xmas cards, wreaths, candles, flower arrangements.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: LaGatta was an illustrator best known for his depictions of glamorous, graceful women. He contributed covers and accompanying article illustrations for the Saturday Evening Post, Ladies' Home Journal, and Life magazine amongst others, and amassed numerous advertising accounts. LaGatta moved from New York to California, settling in Santa Monica circa 1942. He taught at Art Center in Pasadena from 1956 to his death in 1977. Registration Card, in circa 1942 LaGatta lived at 326 Adelaide Dr. in Santa Monica.
Photograph of a hanging wreath encircling a name plate affixed to a door. The plate reads, "J. LaGatta" - standing for John LaGatta - magazine illustrator. (Image is upside down.)

title_tesim: "Merry Widow" production with Natalie Garrotto, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, possibly 1960
caption_tesim: Handwritten on edge of negative: 9
Handwritten on Gevaert film box: Merry Widow, New Moon.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera singers performing in “The Merry Widow,” Act 2. Singer in the role of the widow Sonia Sadoya (Hanna Glawari) stands center stage. She and the other female performers (including Natalie Garrotto portraying Natalie (Valencienne), seated at the table on the left) wear the traditional clothing of a fictional Slavic country, including kokoshnik-inspired headdresses, peasant blouses, full skirts with aprons and waist cinchers. The men wear tuxedos and military dress uniforms. Cast members stand and behind her as she sings. The setting is a garden at Hannah’s home. Scenery flats representing trees, a faux brick wall and a lattice-work structure all contribute to the outdoor setting. Additional props include a rattan table, chairs and settee. Photographed from the balcony, providing a view of the stage, musicians in the orchestra pit, and audience members.
Possibly related to the article, “Civic Group Will Present ‘Merry Widow’,” Los Angeles Times, 12 Jun. 1960: WS5.
English language adaptations of “The Merry Widow” include several name changes. Here, names from the original Viennese work are noted in parentheses after the variation.

title_tesim: “Elisir d’amore” production with June Moss, Memorial Greek Amphitheatre, 1949-1958
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SM Civic Opera, Pagliacci, Elixir, Cavalleria.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singers performing in a production of “L’Elisir d’amore.” At center, Adina (portrayed by June Moss) sings with an unidentified male performing in the role of Belcore. Cast members dressed as peasants and soldiers stand in the background. The setting is a village square in Basque country, late 18th century. Scenery includes a painted backdrop with rolling hills and trees, and set pieces painted to look like stone architecture,  one of which stands in for a cantina complete with a table and chairs and a cask of wine (presumably). Beyond the stage, the conductor and musicians can be viewed at ground level, while audience members are observed in the stands at foreground. Opera performed at Santa Monica High School Amphitheatre.
In the Adelbert Bartlett collection, June Moss is documented in Santa Monica Civic Opera productions from 1949 to 1958.

title_tesim: “Elisir d’amore” production with Natalie Garrotto, Memorial Greek Amphitheatre, 1949-1958
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SM Civic Opera, Pagliacci, Elixir, Cavalleria.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Opera singers performing in “L’Elisir d’amore.” The stage is filled with cast members dressed as Basque villagers. At center, a singer portraying Dr. Dulcamara stands on an elaborately decorated wagon, holding a bottle of elixir. His assistant, wearing a pith helmet and holding a crop, stands next to the wagon. Portions of background sets in the forms of houses and trees can be seen behind the players. Among the performers is Natalie Garrotto, likely playing the role of Giannetta (peasant girl). She wears ballet flats, full skirt with floral embellishments, and a white shawl. Photographed at Santa Monica High School Amphitheatre.
June Moss was in this production. In the Adelbert Bartlett collection, June Moss is documented in Santa Monica Civic Opera productions from 1949 to 1958.

title_tesim: “Cavalleria Rusticana" production with Florence Timmerhoff and June Moss, Barnum Hall, Santa Monica, 1952
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve (image ark no. 21198/zz002d6x5c): Cavalleria. May 11/52. Porta. Timmerhoff.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: Santa Monica Civic Opera performance of in “Cavalleria Rusticana.” Cast members in Italian peasant costumes take up the stage. Several men (including a soldier) stand behind rows of kneeling women in prayer pose. Florence Timmerhoff, portraying Lola, stands at the fore and sings. The setting is a 19th century Sicilian village. Painted backdrops and sets depict stone and brick architecture surrounding a village square. At opposing ends of the stage are a cantina and a church. Beyond the stage, musicians are viewed in the orchestra pit at foreground. Cast members include June Moss, kneeling fourth from right. Opera performed at Barnum Hall, Santa Monica.

title_tesim: “Elisir d’amore” production with June Moss, Memorial Greek Amphitheatre, 1949-1958
caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: SM Civic Opera, Pagliacci, Elixir, Cavalleria.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: In the Adelbert Bartlett collection, June Moss is documented in Santa Monica Civic Opera productions from 1949 to 1958.

Opera singers performing in a production of “L’Elisir d’amore.” June Moss, in the role of Adina, sings with a male performer portraying Dr. Dulcamara (wearing a top hat, waist coat,  tailcoat, breeches and boots). A soldier is seated at a table behind them while cast members dressed as soldiers, peasants and villagers mill about. The setting is a village square in Basque country, late 18th century. Scenery includes a painted backdrop with rolling hills and trees, and set pieces painted to look like stone architecture. Santa Monica High School Amphitheatre.

caption_tesim: Text from negative sleeve: 2985. Oldsmobile auto made in 1909 cost $5500. Photo 2/2/39. Car stored 25 years "good as new" in 1939.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: Adelbert Bartlett Papers. Department of Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library, UCLA.
description_tesim: The car was purchased by C. Standlee Martin (an Oldsmobile dealer based in Long Beach) from its original owner. The car's license plate suggests that it was on display at the Golden Gate Exposition in 1939. For more information on this car, see the article, "Ancient Autos Resurrected: General Motors Hosts to Horseless Carriage Organization," Los Angeles Times, 12 Mar. 1939:E3.

Photograph of a 1909 Oldsmobile Limited being driven on a street. The driver is a man wearing a shirt, jacket, pants and flat cap. He is seated behind a steering wheel that is situated on the right side of the vehicle. The luxury auto has a large wheel base and rides on massive spoke wheels. The tall chassis requires two running boards to climb into the car. A spare tire rests atop the lower running board and is attached to the driver side door with metal bars and straps. All trim, lantern side lights and headlights are probably brass (standard for this vehicle). License plate frame reads: “C. Stanlee Martin Long Beach.” License plate reads: “California World’s Fair 39 3Y954.” In the background, an electricity tower sits at the edge of a vacant lot, giving way to what looks like a residential neighborhood in the distance. Location unidentified, but possibly Long Beach (where the car’s owner worked and lived).
For related clipping see "Relation" note.

Prior to the Model T, most automobiles had right-side steering wheels even though Americans have always driven on the right side of the road. Henry Ford altered the design, making left-steering standard with mass production of the Model T.
